original_abstract: This article examines the transformative impact of the Circle of Concerned African Women Theologians on the landscape of higher theological education across the African continent. Historically, theological academia in Africa has been characterized by significant gender disparities, shaped by the overlapping influences of colonialism, race, and class. In response, the Circle emerged as a vital Pan-African movement dedicated to dismantling these patriarchal structures and fostering an environment of inclusivity and intersectional solidarity. Utilizing African Women’s Theology as a theoretical framework and employing a qualitative methodological approach, the study analyzes the organization's efforts to integrate gender-sensitive curricula and provide essential mentorship for emerging female scholars. The findings highlight the Circle's historical journey and its enduring contributions to promoting gender equality within African theological discourse, ultimately demonstrating how the organization has successfully cultivated a new generation of African women theologians and reshaped the academic study of religion.
humanized_abstract: ABSTRACT This study examines the transformation achieved by Circle of Concerned African Women Theologians (CODESRIA) in the higher institutions of theological learning in Africa. It investigates gender imbalance at the institutions of higher theological learning in Africa in a bid to establish how gender has been played out there over time. This imbalance was occasioned by a multiplicity of factors, most especially colonialism and its attendant effects of racism and classism, among others, which the Circle of Concerned African Women Theologians fought a struggle of liberating women in Africa from the firm grip of men. Drawing on the theoretical lens of African Women’s Theology, using qualitative method of analysis in investigating the development of women in higher institutions of theological learning and challenging discriminatory policies and attitudes against women at such institutions for development, this study analyses the work of Circle of Concerned African Women Theologians, CODESRIA, in bringing about gender balance in institutions of higher theological learning in Africa, describing how women, through the work of CODESRIA, over the years, have become members of the theology faculty through the introduction of feminist theology into the curriculum, which aided their development, while mentoring students’ female liberation in Africa’s higher institutions of theological learning was facilitated and enabled by CODESRIA and through these among other efforts, a new generation of women, today occupy significant positions in higher institutions of Theology faculties in Africa and so also the study establishes that women, in Africa’s higher institutions of theological learning had their studies in Theology facilitated by CODESRIA by recommending women into various higher faculties and schools to study Theology thus enhancing the study of religion for women and men alike.
